content: Eight dipoles of charges of magnitude $e$ each are placed inside a cube. The total electric flux coming out of the cube will be
explanation:
Net charges of one dipole $=-e+e=0$

Net charge of 8 dipoles $=8 \times 0=0$

$\Rightarrow$ Net charge inside cube $=0=q$

By Gauss' law,

Total flux emerging from surface $=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{0}{\varepsilon_0}=0$

content: The work done in moving a charge particle of charge $2 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ between two points having potential difference of 36 V is
explanation:
To calculate the work done in moving a charged particle with a charge of $2 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{C}$ across a potential difference of 36 V, we use the formula:

$ W = q \cdot \Delta V $

Where:

$ W $ is the work done,

$ q = 2 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{C} $ (charge of the particle),

$ \Delta V = 36 \, \text{V} $ (potential difference).

Substituting the given values into the equation, we get:

$ W = 2 \times 10^{-8} \times 36 $

$ W = 72 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{J} $

Converting the result to microjoules ($\mu J$):

$ W = 0.72 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J} $

$ W = 0.72 \, \mu \text{J} $

The work done in moving the charge is $0.72 \, \mu \text{J}$.

content: Three charges, each of $+4 \mu \mathrm{C}$, are placed at the corners $A, B, C$ of a square $A B C D$ of side 1 m . What is the magnitude (in $\mathrm{NC}^{-1}$ ) of electric field at point $O$ towards point $D$ ?
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &E_A \text { cancels } E_C \text {. }\\ &\text { At } \begin{aligned} E_B & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{\left(\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{2 q}{d^2} \\ & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6}}{1^2} \\ & =72 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}, \text { towards } D \\ & =72000 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: Two point charges of $+3 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-3 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at a certain distance apart from each other. Electric potential at a distance 60 cm from the mid-point of dipole on axial line is 150 V , then the value of dipole length is
explanation:
Given:

Electric potential, $ V = 150 \, \text{V} $

Distance from the mid-point of the dipole on the axial line, $ r = 60 \, \text{cm} = 0.6 \, \text{m} $

The formula for the electric potential $ V $ on the axial line is:

$ V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p \cos \theta}{r^2} $

For the axial line, $ \theta = 0^\circ $.

Thus, the potential becomes:

$ V = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{p \cdot 1}{r^2} $

Substitute the given values:

$ 150 = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times p}{(0.6)^2} $

Solving for $ p $:

$ p = \frac{150 \times 0.36}{9 \times 10^9} $

So,

$ p = 6 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{Cm} $

The dipole moment $ p = 2a \cdot q $.

Thus,

$ 2a = \frac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{q} $

Given the charge $ q = 3 \, \mu\text{C} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{C} $:

$ 2a = \frac{6 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} $

This simplifies to:

$ 2a = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} $

The length of the dipole, $ 2a $, is hence $ 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} $.

content: Electric field in the region is given by $$\mathbf{E}=\left(M / x^4\right) \hat{\mathbf{i}}$$, then the correct expression for the potential in the region is (assume potential at infinity is zero)
explanation: $$V=-\int_\limits{\infty}^x E \cdot d r=-\int_\limits{\infty}^x M / x^4 d x=\frac{M}{3 x^3}$$

content: The net electric force on a charge of $$+3 \mu \mathrm{C}$$ at the mid-point on the line joining two charges of magnitude $$+2 \mu \mathrm{C}$$ and $$-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$$ separated by the distance of $$6 \mathrm{~mm}$$, is
explanation:
From Coulomb's law

Force due to same charge is repulsive and that due to opposite is attractive.

$$F=K\left[\frac{2 \times 3}{3^2}-\frac{2 \times 3}{3^2}\right]=0$$

content: A hollow sphere of radius $$0.1 \mathrm{~m}$$ has a charge of $$5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$$. The potential at a distance of 5 $$\mathrm{cm}$$ from the centre of the sphere is $$\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-2}\right)$$
An electric charge does not have which of the following properties?
explanation:
The correct option that represents a property not associated with an electric charge is Option D: Circular line of force.

Let's examine each option closely to understand why:

Option A: Total charge conservation - This principle states that the total charge in an isolated system remains constant. This is a fundamental property of electric charge, governed by the law of conservation of charge. It means that in any process, the sum of all the electric charges before the process equals the sum of all the electric charges after the process, indicating that electric charge cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred from one body to another.

Option B: Quantisation of charge - The quantization of charge means that the charge on an object is always an integer multiple of the elementary charge (e), where \(e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \) Coulombs. No matter how small, a charge can’t be a fraction of this elementary charge, thus demonstrating that charge is quantized.

Option C: Two types of charge - There are indeed two types of electric charges: positive and negative. This dichotomy is foundational to the structure of electromagnetism, where like charges repel each other and opposite charges attract each other. This property is fundamental to the behavior of charged particles and underlies practically all electric phenomena.

Option D: Circular line of force - Electric lines of force or electric field lines emanating from a charged particle are radial and straight, originating from positive charges and ending at negative charges. They are not circular in nature under normal circumstances. The concept of circular lines of force is more appropriately linked to magnetic fields around a current-carrying conductor or the magnetic field of a bar magnet, not to electric charges directly. Therefore, Option D is a property that does not belong to electric charges.
